title: PIC — Federation Bridge Entry
description: How a PIC transaction starts. An external identity (here OAuth/OIDC, but the same pattern applies to SPIFFE, DID/VC, X.509) is translated into PCA_0 — the root Causal Authority that anchors every subsequent hop. The Federation Bridge extracts the immutable origin principal p_0 from the credential (sub claim → p_0) and the initial operation set ops_0 from the credential's authority claims (scope → ops_0), then signs PCA_0 with executor binding to the first executor E_0. From this point on, p_0 is immutable and ops can only monotonically restrict.

steps.label: POST /token (auth code)
steps.explanation: Alice authenticates at her organization's OIDC Provider via a standard OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code exchange. This is the external authentication step — PIC does not replace OAuth/OIDC; it consumes the resulting credential to anchor a transaction. Any source that can carry an immutable principal identifier and an authority set works equally (SPIFFE SVID, DID/VC, X.509).
annotations: PIC is identity-system-agnostic. The source credential here is OIDC, but pic-spec.md §6.5.5 and §6.5.6 cover SPIFFE, DID/VC, and X.509 as equivalent entry points.
Only two facts must be extractable from the credential: an immutable principal identifier (becomes p_0) and an authority set (becomes ops_0).
The credential is consumed once at the boundary and never propagated further — only the derived PCA_0 flows into the trust plane.

steps.label: ID Token (JWT)
steps.explanation: The OIDC Provider returns an ID Token. Two claims will be load-bearing for PIC: sub (the principal identifier — will become p_0) and scope (the authority set — will become ops_0). Everything else (aud, iat, exp) is OIDC bookkeeping and does not propagate into the PIC trust plane.
annotations: sub MUST be stable across this user's sessions. PIC's whole security model rests on p_0 being immutable.
scope grants are typically broader than what any single hop needs — that's expected. ops monotonically restricts as the transaction descends.
PIC does not require the ID Token to be exchangeable, refreshable, or DPoP-bound. It is read once and dropped at the Federation Bridge.

steps.label: GET /api (with ID token)
steps.explanation: Alice's client calls the API gateway (Executor E_0 — the entry point into the trust domain) carrying her ID token. E_0 will not act on the ID token directly; it will hand it to the Federation Bridge, which is the only component authorized to anchor a new PIC transaction. This separation keeps trust-anchor-issuance off the request path of every executor.
annotations: E_0 is just the first hop inside the PIC trust domain. It has no special authority — its role is to be bound as the executor in PCA_0 so that it (and only it) can later present PoC_1 to advance the transaction.
In a typical deployment, E_0 is an API gateway, ingress proxy, or edge service. It is the seam between 'external credential world' and 'PIC causal authority world'.
Forwarding the ID token to the Federation Bridge (next step) can be done out-of-band, via a sidecar, or via a header — implementation choice. The bridge is logically the single anchor point.

steps.label: POST /pic/bootstrap

artifacts.raw_input:
ID Token claims:
  iss = https://oidc.example.com
  sub = alice@example.com
  scope = read:files write:files

E_0 attestation:
  spiffe_id = spiffe://example.com/gateway/e0
artifacts.signing_key_hint: No signing — this is the identity-mapping rule the Federation Bridge applies to derive p_0 and ops_0.

steps.label: 1. Extract p_0 from credential (pic-spec.md §6.5.5)
steps.value:
Source: OIDC ID Token
Rule:   p_0 = ID_Token.sub

p_0 = "alice@example.com"

This value is now FROZEN for the entire transaction.
No subsequent hop, bridge, or policy step is allowed
to change it. Every PCA_i.p_0 MUST equal this value.

Identity mapping by source (pic-spec.md table §6.5.5):
  OAuth/OIDC  → JWT.sub        → p_0
  SPIFFE      → SVID SPIFFE ID  → p_0
  DID/VC      → DID URI         → p_0
  X.509       → cert Subject DN → p_0

The principle: whatever stable identifier the credential
carries IS the origin principal. PIC does not rename it,
translate it, or strip it.

steps.label: 2. Extract ops_0 from credential (pic-spec.md §6.5.5)
steps.value:
Source: OIDC ID Token
Rule:   ops_0 = parsed authority claims

ID_Token.scope = "read:files write:files"

ops_0 = [
  "read:files/*",
  "write:files/*"
]

This is the BROADEST authority alice will ever have in
this transaction. From here, monotonicity is enforced:
  ops_0 ⊇ ops_1 ⊇ ops_2 ⊇ ...

Authority mapping by source:
  OAuth/OIDC  → scope claim
  SPIFFE      → trust-domain policy attached to SPIFFE ID
  DID/VC      → VC credential subject claims
  X.509       → certificate extensions / role attributes

The Federation Bridge MAY apply a local restricting
policy here (e.g., 'OIDC tokens from this issuer never
grant write:billing'). Restriction is allowed; expansion
is not — the bridge cannot grant ops not justified by
the source credential.

steps.label: 3. Bind PCA_0 to E_0
steps.value:
Source: E_0's executor attestation (SPIFFE SVID)
Rule:   executor.binding = attestation identity

executor.binding = "spiffe://example.com/gateway/e0"

Why bind to a specific executor: PCA is non-transferable
by design. If E_0 hands PCA_0 to anyone other than CAT
(via a properly signed PoC_1), the recipient cannot use
it — their attestation will not match executor.binding.

This closes the 'stolen token' attack class: PCA is not
a bearer credential. It is a claim about authority that
is only meaningful when presented by the attested executor.

steps.explanation: E_0 forwards the ID token to the Federation Bridge along with its own executor attestation (a SPIFFE SVID here, but TEE quote / X.509 / VP all work). The Federation Bridge is the single place authorized to mint PCA_0 — concentrating trust-anchor issuance to one component is what makes the rest of the trust plane simple.
annotations: The Federation Bridge MUST validate the source credential against its issuer's keys (here: fetch OIDC JWKS, verify RS256 signature, check iss/aud/exp).
The Federation Bridge MUST validate E_0's executor attestation independently. PCA_0 is bound to E_0; the bridge needs to know who E_0 actually is.
The Federation Bridge MAY consult Governance here to apply ingress policy (e.g., reject if the user is on a deny list). Governance is consulted at issuance, not at every hop.

steps.label: PCA_0 (signed)
steps.explanation: The Federation Bridge signs PCA_0 — the root of the causal chain. Note provenance is null: PCA_0 has no predecessor (it is the predecessor for everything that follows). hop=0. p_0 is now frozen for the entire transaction. The bridge's signature is what every downstream CAT/executor will trace back to when verifying provenance.
annotations: PCA_0 is the only PCA in the entire chain whose provenance is null. Every PCA_i for i>0 MUST reference its predecessor's CAT signature and its predecessor executor's signature.
The transport encoding here is shown as JWT-like for readability, but pic-spec.md uses COSE_Sign1 (CBOR-encoded) on the wire. The 3-part header.payload.signature structure is identical.
Constraints (temporal exp, environment regions) attached at PCA_0 are the OUTER bound. Any successor PCA's constraints MUST be a subset (e.g., later hops can have shorter exp, but never longer).
The Federation Bridge's signing key (kid=fed-bridge-kid-01) is part of the trust domain's known anchor set. Every CAT and executor in this domain knows this key.

steps.label: 200 OK (transaction anchored)
steps.explanation: E_0 now holds PCA_0 and is the active executor for alice's transaction. It returns alice's response. From this point forward, any further hop (E_0 calling a downstream service) follows the Causal Authority Transition flow — see the next scenario.
annotations: The transaction is anchored: p_0 = alice, ops = (read:files/*, write:files/*), executor = E_0.
If E_0 needs to fan out to multiple services, each fan-out call requires its own Causal Authority Transition (one PoC + one new PCA_1 per recipient).
If alice's session ends or PCA_0 expires (1-hour TTL here), the transaction ends. There is no refresh-token equivalent — PCA is short-lived by design.
The _pic envelope shown in the response body is illustrative for this explorer; in practice PIC envelopes are out-of-band (response headers, sidecar metadata, observability data).
